text: Prolonged exercise in the heat causes a rise in body core temperature and loss of body water via sweating, leading to hyperthermia and hypohydration that impair aerobic performance.
Heat acclimation is an effective strategy to mitigate the negative effects of hyperthermia and hypohydration on exercise performance in hot conditions.
Cooling strategies (pre-cooling and per-cooling) can enhance aerobic performance under heat stress by reducing thermal strain.
Hyperhydration before exercise in the heat can help mitigate performance decrements caused by hypohydration.
Exercise-heat stress can cause sodium disturbances and in severe cases exertional heat illness, requiring monitoring of both thermal strain and hydration status.
Systemic and peripheral hemodynamic adjustments during exercise in the heat contribute to fatigue development, as blood flow is redirected to the skin for cooling at the expense of working muscles.
